title: Check if array is sorted
description: Given an array determine if it is sorted in non decreasing order

examples.explanation:
n = 5
Loop i from 0 to 3:
  i=0: Loop j from 1 to 4:
    j=1: arr[0]=1 ≤ arr[1]=2 ✓
    j=2: arr[0]=1 ≤ arr[2]=3 ✓
    j=3: arr[0]=1 ≤ arr[3]=4 ✓
    j=4: arr[0]=1 ≤ arr[4]=5 ✓
  i=1: Loop j from 2 to 4:
    j=2: arr[1]=2 ≤ arr[2]=3 ✓
    j=3: arr[1]=2 ≤ arr[3]=4 ✓
    j=4: arr[1]=2 ≤ arr[4]=5 ✓
  i=2: Loop j from 3 to 4:
    j=3: arr[2]=3 ≤ arr[3]=4 ✓
    j=4: arr[2]=3 ≤ arr[4]=5 ✓
  i=3: Loop j from 4 to 4:
    j=4: arr[3]=4 ≤ arr[4]=5 ✓
All pairs satisfy condition → return True

n = 5
Loop i from 0 to 3:
  i=0: Loop j from 1 to 4:
    j=1: arr[0]=5 > arr[1]=3 ✗ (5 > 3)
    Condition violated → return False immediately
No need to check further pairs

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Compare every pair of elements in the array to check if any element at a later index is smaller than an earlier one. Use nested loops to iterate through all possible pairs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Array Sorted Check
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of checking if an array is sorted in non-decreasing order using nested loops.
ladders.explanation:
The brute force approach to checking if an array is sorted in non-decreasing order involves comparing every possible pair of elements to ensure no element at a later index is smaller than an element at an earlier index. This is the most straightforward method that directly implements the definition of sorted order. Intuitively, if an array is sorted, then for any two indices i and j where i < j, we must have arr[i] ≤ arr[j]. The brute force approach tests this condition for all possible pairs.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Get the length n of the input array.
2. For each index i from 0 to n-2 (inclusive):
   a. For each index j from i+1 to n-1 (inclusive):
      b. If arr[i] > arr[j], return False immediately (array is not sorted).
3. If all pairs satisfy arr[i] ≤ arr[j], return True.

Time complexity: O(n²) because we have nested loops iterating through all pairs. For an array of size n, there are n(n-1)/2 pairs, which is quadratic.
Space complexity: O(1) as we only use a few variables regardless of input size.

Connection to L1: This full solution builds upon the key subroutine of comparing two elements, which is the fundamental operation repeated throughout the nested loops.

Common mistakes:
1. Off-by-one errors in loop bounds (e.g., going to n instead of n-1).
2. Returning True too early before checking all pairs.
3. Confusing non-decreasing (≤) with strictly increasing (<).

Real code pattern (Python-style pseudocode):
def is_sorted_bruteforce(arr):
    n = len(arr)
    for i in range(n-1):
        for j in range(i+1, n):
            if arr[i] > arr[j]:
                return False
    return True

examples.trace:
n = 5
Loop i from 0 to 3:
  i=0: Loop j from 1 to 4:
    j=1: arr[0]=1 ≤ arr[1]=2 ✓
    j=2: arr[0]=1 ≤ arr[2]=3 ✓
    j=3: arr[0]=1 ≤ arr[3]=4 ✓
    j=4: arr[0]=1 ≤ arr[4]=5 ✓
  i=1: Loop j from 2 to 4:
    j=2: arr[1]=2 ≤ arr[2]=3 ✓
    j=3: arr[1]=2 ≤ arr[3]=4 ✓
    j=4: arr[1]=2 ≤ arr[4]=5 ✓
  i=2: Loop j from 3 to 4:
    j=3: arr[2]=3 ≤ arr[3]=4 ✓
    j=4: arr[2]=3 ≤ arr[4]=5 ✓
  i=3: Loop j from 4 to 4:
    j=4: arr[3]=4 ≤ arr[4]=5 ✓
All pairs satisfy condition → return True

n = 5
Loop i from 0 to 3:
  i=0: Loop j from 1 to 4:
    j=1: arr[0]=5 > arr[1]=3 ✗ (5 > 3)
    Condition violated → return False immediately
No need to check further pairs

ladders.title: Key Subroutine — Compare All Pairs in Array
ladders.desc: Implement nested loops to compare every element with all elements that come after it.
ladders.explanation:
The key subroutine in the brute force approach is the nested loop structure that generates all possible pairs (i, j) where i < j. This is the engine that drives the entire algorithm. Intuitively, to check if an array is sorted, we need to verify that no 'inversion' exists - where an earlier element is greater than a later element. The nested loops systematically generate all such possible inversions to check.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize n = length of array.
2. Outer loop: For i from 0 to n-2 (we don't need to check the last element as the starting point).
3. Inner loop: For j from i+1 to n-1 (all elements after position i).
4. For each (i, j) pair, we would compare arr[i] and arr[j] (though the actual comparison happens at the next level).

Time complexity: O(n²) for generating all pairs. Space complexity: O(1).

Connection to L0: This subroutine provides the pair generation mechanism that the full solution uses to check each pair.

Connection to L2: This builds upon the core logic of comparing two elements, applying it systematically to all relevant pairs.

Common mistakes:
1. Starting inner loop at i instead of i+1 (would compare elements with themselves).
2. Going to n in outer loop instead of n-1 (would cause index out of bounds in inner loop).
3. Not handling empty or single-element arrays correctly.

Real code pattern (Python-style pseudocode):
def generate_all_pairs(arr):
    n = len(arr)
    pairs = []
    for i in range(n-1):
        for j in range(i+1, n):
            pairs.append((i, j))
    return pairs
# This returns all index pairs; in actual solution we'd compare directly

examples.trace:
n = 3
Outer loop i from 0 to 1:
  i=0: Inner loop j from 1 to 2:
    j=1: Add pair (0,1)
    j=2: Add pair (0,2)
  i=1: Inner loop j from 2 to 2:
    j=2: Add pair (1,2)
Total pairs: (0,1), (0,2), (1,2)

n = 4
Outer loop i from 0 to 2:
  i=0: j from 1 to 3: (0,1), (0,2), (0,3)
  i=1: j from 2 to 3: (1,2), (1,3)
  i=2: j from 3 to 3: (2,3)
Total 6 pairs

ladders.title: Core Logic — Compare Two Elements for Sorted Order
ladders.desc: Check if an element at an earlier index is less than or equal to an element at a later index.
ladders.explanation:
The core logic of the brute force approach is comparing two elements to determine if they violate the sorted order condition. For an array to be sorted in non-decreasing order, for any two indices i and j where i < j, we must have arr[i] ≤ arr[j]. This comparison is the fundamental check that gets repeated for every pair. Intuitively, if we find any pair where arr[i] > arr[j], we've found a 'violation' or 'inversion' that proves the array is not sorted.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Take two indices i and j where i < j.
2. Retrieve elements arr[i] and arr[j].
3. Compare: if arr[i] > arr[j], return False (violation found).
4. Otherwise, the pair satisfies the condition.

Time complexity: O(1) for a single comparison. Space complexity: O(1).

Connection to L1: This comparison is what we perform for each pair generated by the nested loops.

Connection to L3: This builds upon the basic operation of comparing two numbers, applying it to the specific context of sorted order checking.

Common mistakes:
1. Using < instead of ≤ (would incorrectly flag equal elements as unsorted).
2. Comparing arr[j] > arr[i] instead of arr[i] > arr[j] (logic reversal).
3. Not handling the case where i ≥ j (though in proper usage i < j should be guaranteed).

Real code pattern (Python-style pseudocode):
def is_pair_sorted(arr, i, j):
    # Assumes i < j
    if arr[i] > arr[j]:
        return False  # Violation found
    return True       # This pair is okay

examples.trace:
arr[0] = 5
arr[1] = 3
Check: 5 > 3? Yes → Condition violated
Return False

arr[0] = 2
arr[1] = 5
Check: 2 > 5? No → Condition satisfied
Return True

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Indexing and Element Access
ladders.desc: Access elements in an array using indices and understand index relationships.
ladders.explanation:
This building block focuses on accessing array elements using indices and understanding the relationship between indices. In the brute force approach, we need to access arr[i] and arr[j] where i and j are indices with i < j. Understanding how array indexing works is crucial for implementing the comparison logic correctly. Intuitively, an array is a contiguous block of memory where each element has a numerical index starting from 0. The index represents the element's position, and we can access any element in constant time using its index.

Step-by-step concept:
1. Arrays are zero-indexed: first element is at index 0, second at index 1, etc.
2. To access element at index i: use arr[i] notation.
3. Valid indices range from 0 to n-1 where n is array length.
4. The relationship i < j means element at index i comes before element at index j in the array.

Time complexity: O(1) to access any element by index. Space complexity: O(1).

Connection to L2: This provides the mechanism to retrieve the two elements needed for comparison.

Connection to L4: This builds upon the basic operation of comparing values, adding the context of retrieving those values from an array structure.

Common mistakes:
1. Using 1-based indexing (thinking first element is at index 1).
2. Accessing indices outside valid range (causing index out of bounds error).
3. Confusing array values with indices.

Real code pattern (Python-style pseudocode):
# Accessing array elements
arr = [10, 20, 30, 40]
first_element = arr[0]    # 10
second_element = arr[1]   # 20
last_element = arr[3]     # 40 (since length is 4, last index is 3)

# Example of accessing with variables
i = 1
j = 3
element_i = arr[i]  # 20
element_j = arr[j]  # 40

examples.trace:
Array has 4 elements: indices 0, 1, 2, 3
arr[0] accesses first element: 5
arr[2] accesses third element: 3

Array has 3 elements: indices 0, 1, 2
arr[1] accesses second element: 200
Accessing same index twice gives same value

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Determine the relationship between two numbers using comparison operators.
ladders.explanation:
This basic operation involves comparing two numbers to determine their relationship. In the context of checking if an array is sorted, we specifically need to check if one number is greater than another. The comparison operators (>, <, ≥, ≤, ==, ≠) are fundamental building blocks of programming logic. Intuitively, comparing numbers allows us to make decisions and control program flow based on quantitative relationships.

Step-by-step concept:
1. Take two numbers a and b.
2. Apply comparison operator:
   - a > b: Is a greater than b?
   - a < b: Is a less than b?
   - a ≥ b: Is a greater than or equal to b?
   - a ≤ b: Is a less than or equal to b?
3. The result is a boolean value: True or False.

For sorted array checking, we use the > comparison to detect violations: if a > b where a comes before b in the array, then we have a violation.

Time complexity: O(1). Space complexity: O(1).

Connection to L3: This comparison is applied to the values retrieved from array indexing.

Connection to L5: This builds upon the foundation of understanding numbers and their relationships.

Common mistakes:
1. Confusing > with ≥ (strictly greater vs greater or equal).
2. Writing comparison backward (b > a instead of a > b).
3. Not understanding that comparison returns a boolean, not a number.

Real code pattern (Python-style pseudocode):
# Basic number comparisons
a = 5
b = 3
result1 = a > b   # True, because 5 > 3
result2 = a < b   # False, because 5 is not < 3
result3 = a >= b  # True, because 5 ≥ 3
result4 = a <= b  # False, because 5 is not ≤ 3
result5 = a == b  # False, because 5 ≠ 3

examples.trace:
7 is greater than 4
7 > 4 evaluates to True

2 is not greater than 5
2 > 5 evaluates to False

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Numbers and Order
ladders.desc: Grasp the basic concept that numbers have magnitude and can be ordered.
ladders.explanation:
This foundational concept establishes that numbers have magnitude (size/value) and can be compared to determine which is larger or smaller. This is the most basic prerequisite for any sorting-related algorithm. Intuitively, just as we can say one object is heavier than another or one person is taller than another, numbers have this inherent property of order. For example, 5 is greater than 3, and 2 is less than 7. This ordering forms the basis of what it means for an array to be 'sorted' - the numbers are arranged from smallest to largest (or non-decreasing order).

Step-by-step concept:
1. Numbers represent quantities: 0, 1, 2, 3, etc.
2. Numbers have relative size: 5 is bigger than 3, 10 is bigger than 5.
3. We can arrange numbers in order: smallest to largest or largest to smallest.
4. Negative numbers are less than positive numbers, and more negative numbers are less than less negative numbers.
5. Equal numbers have the same magnitude.

This foundation doesn't involve code yet - it's the mathematical understanding that enables all the higher-level concepts.

Connection to L4: This understanding enables us to use comparison operators meaningfully.

Common misunderstandings:
1. Thinking of numbers as just symbols without magnitude.
2. Not understanding that negative numbers can be compared (e.g., -5 is less than -3).
3. Confusing digit count with magnitude (e.g., thinking 100 is less than 99 because it has more digits).

Real-world analogy: Imagine arranging books by thickness - you can compare any two books and say which is thicker. Similarly, numbers can be compared to determine which is larger.

examples.trace:
Compare all numbers:
1 is smallest
2 is next smallest (greater than 1)
5 is next (greater than 2)
8 is largest (greater than 5)
So ordered from smallest to largest: 1, 2, 5, 8

Negative numbers are less than positive numbers
-3 is negative, 0 is neither negative nor positive but is greater than negatives
Therefore -3 < 0

approaches.name: Linear Scan O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through the array once and compare each element with its next neighbor to ensure it is not greater. Stop early if any violation is found.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check if Array is Sorted Using Linear Scan
ladders.desc: Complete solution to determine if an array is sorted in non-decreasing order using a single pass through the array.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that for an array to be sorted in non-decreasing order, every element must be less than or equal to its next neighbor. If we find any element that is greater than the next element, the array is not sorted. This works because sortedness is a transitive property—if each adjacent pair is in order, the entire sequence is ordered. The algorithm iterates through the array from the first element to the second-to-last element, comparing each element with the next one. If any comparison fails (current > next), we immediately return false. If we complete the loop without finding any violations, we return true. This is efficient because we stop early at the first violation, making best-case O(1) time when the first two elements are out of order, and worst-case O(n) when the array is sorted or the violation is at the end. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete function that calls the comparison logic. Common mistakes include: 1) Off-by-one errors in loop bounds (going to last element instead of second-to-last), 2) Using strict less-than instead of less-than-or-equal for non-decreasing order, 3) Not handling empty or single-element arrays correctly (they are trivially sorted). Real code pattern: def is_sorted(arr): if len(arr) <= 1: return True; for i in range(len(arr)-1): if arr[i] > arr[i+1]: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: Array length is 5, not <=1, so continue. Step 2: i=0, compare arr[0]=1 and arr[1]=2, 1 <= 2, continue. Step 3: i=1, compare 2 and 3, 2 <= 3, continue. Step 4: i=2, compare 3 and 4, 3 <= 4, continue. Step 5: i=3, compare 4 and 5, 4 <= 5, continue. Step 6: Loop ends, return true.
Step 1: Array length is 4, not <=1, so continue. Step 2: i=0, compare arr[0]=5 and arr[1]=3, 5 > 3, violation found, return false immediately.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compare Adjacent Elements in Array
ladders.desc: Implement the core comparison logic that checks if any adjacent pair violates the non-decreasing order.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that sortedness can be verified by examining each consecutive pair of elements. If all pairs satisfy the condition (current <= next), the array is sorted. This sub-routine focuses on performing this check efficiently by iterating through pairs and stopping at the first violation. It works because it breaks down the global property (entire array sorted) into local properties (each adjacent pair sorted). The algorithm: given an array, loop from index 0 to len(arr)-2, compare arr[i] with arr[i+1], if arr[i] > arr[i+1] return false, else continue. After loop, return true. Time complexity is O(n) in worst case, O(1) in best case when violation is early. Space is O(1). This feeds into L0 by being the main logic inside the full solution. Common mistakes: 1) Missing the edge case of empty/single-element arrays (handled in L0), 2) Incorrect loop condition causing index out of bounds, 3) Using wrong comparison operator. Real code pattern: def check_pairs(arr): for i in range(len(arr)-1): if arr[i] > arr[i+1]: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, compare 2 and 4, 2 <= 4, continue. Step 2: i=1, compare 4 and 6, 4 <= 6, continue. Step 3: i=2, compare 6 and 8, 6 <= 8, continue. Step 4: Loop ends, return true.
Step 1: i=0, compare 3 and 1, 3 > 1, violation found, return false immediately.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Early Termination on First Violation
ladders.desc: Learn to stop processing as soon as a sortedness violation is detected.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that once we find one pair out of order, we don't need to check the rest of the array—the array is definitely not sorted. This optimization reduces average time complexity. It works by breaking out of the loop immediately when a violation is found, rather than checking all pairs unnecessarily. The algorithm: iterate through indices, compare current and next element, if current > next, return false and exit; otherwise continue. If loop completes without early exit, return true. Time: best-case O(1) (first pair violates), worst-case O(n) (all pairs valid or violation at end). Space: O(1). This connects to L1 as the efficiency mechanism within the pair-checking routine. Common mistakes: 1) Forgetting to return false immediately and instead setting a flag (less efficient), 2) Not understanding that early termination doesn't change worst-case complexity but improves practical performance. Real code pattern: for i in range(n-1): if arr[i] > arr[i+1]: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, compare 1 and 5, 1 <= 5, continue. Step 2: i=1, compare 5 and 3, 5 > 3, violation found, return false immediately (no need to check i=2 or i=3).
Step 1: i=0, compare 2 and 3, 2 <= 3, continue. Step 2: i=1, compare 3 and 4, 3 <= 4, continue. Step 3: i=2, compare 4 and 5, 4 <= 5, continue. Step 4: Loop ends, return true (checked all pairs).

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterate Through Array Indices with Safe Bounds
ladders.desc: Practice looping through an array while accessing consecutive elements without index errors.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that to compare each element with its next neighbor, we need to access indices i and i+1, so we must stop the loop before the last element to avoid out-of-bounds access. This ensures we only compare valid pairs. It works by setting the loop range from 0 to len(arr)-2 (inclusive), or equivalently while i < len(arr)-1. The algorithm: given array arr, for i from 0 to len(arr)-2, we can safely access arr[i] and arr[i+1]. Time: O(n) for the loop, space: O(1). This feeds into L2 by providing the correct iteration framework for early termination. Common mistakes: 1) Looping to len(arr)-1 and accessing arr[i+1] at last iteration (causes index error), 2) Using while loop without careful condition, 3) Not adjusting for zero-based indexing. Real code pattern: n = len(arr); for i in range(n-1): # use arr[i] and arr[i+1] safely.

examples.input: Array of length 4: [a, b, c, d]
examples.trace: Step 1: len(arr)=4, so loop i from 0 to 2 (since 4-1=3, range(3) gives 0,1,2). Step 2: At i=0, access arr[0]=a and arr[1]=b. Step 3: At i=1, access arr[1]=b and arr[2]=c. Step 4: At i=2, access arr[2]=c and arr[3]=d. Step 5: Loop ends, no index error.
Step 1: len(arr)=2, so loop i from 0 to 0 (since 2-1=1, range(1) gives 0). Step 2: At i=0, access arr[0]=x and arr[1]=y. Step 3: Loop ends, safe access.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare Two Numbers for Order
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the fundamental comparison between two elements.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that sorting is based on pairwise relationships: for non-decreasing order, each element must be less than or equal to the next. This operation checks if a given pair (a, b) satisfies a <= b. It works by using comparison operators: if a > b, the pair is out of order; otherwise, it's in order. This is the atomic unit of the sortedness check. The algorithm: given two numbers a and b, return true if a <= b, false if a > b. Time and space: O(1). This connects to L3 as the operation performed at each iteration. Common mistakes: 1) Confusing non-decreasing (<=) with strictly increasing (<), 2) Not handling equal numbers correctly, 3) Using wrong operator due to misunderstanding order. Real code pattern: def is_ordered(a, b): return a <= b.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compare 3 and 5. Step 2: Check if 3 <= 5. Step 3: 3 is less than 5, so condition is true.
Step 1: Compare 7 and 4. Step 2: Check if 7 <= 4. Step 3: 7 is greater than 4, so condition is false.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Access Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Learn how to retrieve elements from an array using their positions.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and we can access any element if we know its index (position). This is fundamental because the linear scan approach requires looking at each element in sequence. It works by using zero-based indexing: the first element is at index 0, second at index 1, etc. For an array arr, arr[i] gives the element at position i. The algorithm: given an array and an index i, return arr[i]. Time: O(1) access, space: O(1). This feeds into L4 as we need to access pairs of elements to compare them. Common mistakes: 1) Using one-based indexing (common beginner error), 2) Accessing index out of bounds (e.g., index equal to or greater than array length), 3) Confusing index with value. Real code pattern: element = arr[index].

examples.trace: Step 1: Array has elements at indices: 0->10, 1->20, 2->30. Step 2: Access index 1. Step 3: Return arr[1] which is 20.
Step 1: Array indices: 0->5, 1->8, 2->2, 3->9. Step 2: Access index 0. Step 3: Return arr[0] which is 5.

approaches.name: Recursive Divide and Conquer O(n)
approaches.summary: Recursively split the array into halves and check if each half is sorted, then combine results by verifying the boundary between halves. This mimics merge sort logic without sorting.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(log n) due to recursion stack

ladders.title: Full Problem — Recursive Divide and Conquer Check for Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Complete solution using recursive divide and conquer to check if array is sorted in non-decreasing order.
ladders.explanation: This approach mimics the divide phase of merge sort without actually sorting. The intuition is that an array is sorted if and only if: (1) its left half is sorted, (2) its right half is sorted, and (3) the last element of the left half is ≤ the first element of the right half. We recursively split the array until we reach base cases (empty or single-element arrays, which are trivially sorted), then combine results while checking the boundary condition. The algorithm works because it decomposes the global sorted property into local properties that can be verified independently and combined. Step-by-step: 1. Define recursive function isSorted(arr, start, end) returning boolean. 2. Base cases: if start ≥ end (empty or single element), return true. 3. Calculate mid = (start + end) // 2. 4. Recursively check left half: leftSorted = isSorted(arr, start, mid). 5. Recursively check right half: rightSorted = isSorted(arr, mid+1, end). 6. If both halves are sorted, check boundary: arr[mid] ≤ arr[mid+1]. 7. Return leftSorted AND rightSorted AND boundaryCheck. Time complexity is O(n) because each element is visited once during boundary checks (similar to merge sort's combine phase but without merging). Space complexity is O(log n) for recursion stack depth. Connection to L1: This uses the boundary checking subroutine from L1 as a key component. Common mistakes: Forgetting to check boundary condition, incorrect base cases (e.g., treating two-element arrays as base), or not handling empty arrays. Real code pattern: def isSorted(arr, start, end): if start >= end: return True; mid = (start + end) // 2; left = isSorted(arr, start, mid); right = isSorted(arr, mid+1, end); return left and right and (arr[mid] <= arr[mid+1]).

examples.trace: Call isSorted(arr, 0, 4). start=0, end=4, mid=2. Recursively check left half: isSorted(arr, 0, 2). start=0, end=2, mid=1. Check left-left: isSorted(arr, 0, 1). start=0, end=1, mid=0. Check left-left-left: isSorted(arr, 0, 0) returns true (base case). Check left-left-right: isSorted(arr, 1, 1) returns true. Boundary check: arr[0]=1 ≤ arr[1]=2 → true. So left-left returns true. Check left-right: isSorted(arr, 2, 2) returns true. Boundary check for left half: arr[1]=2 ≤ arr[2]=3 → true. So left half returns true. Recursively check right half: isSorted(arr, 3, 4). start=3, end=4, mid=3. Check right-left: isSorted(arr, 3, 3) returns true. Check right-right: isSorted(arr, 4, 4) returns true. Boundary check: arr[3]=4 ≤ arr[4]=5 → true. So right half returns true. Final boundary check: arr[2]=3 ≤ arr[3]=4 → true. Return true.

Call isSorted(arr, 0, 4). start=0, end=4, mid=2. Recursively check left half: isSorted(arr, 0, 2). start=0, end=2, mid=1. Check left-left: isSorted(arr, 0, 1). start=0, end=1, mid=0. Check left-left-left: isSorted(arr, 0, 0) returns true. Check left-left-right: isSorted(arr, 1, 1) returns true. Boundary check: arr[0]=5 ≤ arr[1]=4 → false. So left-left returns false. Since left-left is false, left half returns false (no need to check further due to AND logic). Recursively check right half: isSorted(arr, 3, 4). start=3, end=4, mid=3. Check right-left: isSorted(arr, 3, 3) returns true. Check right-right: isSorted(arr, 4, 4) returns true. Boundary check: arr[3]=2 ≤ arr[4]=1 → false. So right half returns false. Final boundary check not reached due to early false. Return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Boundary Check Between Sorted Halves
ladders.desc: Check if two sorted halves can be combined into a sorted whole by verifying boundary elements.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the divide and conquer approach. The intuition is that if you have two arrays that are individually sorted, the combined array is sorted if and only if the last element of the left half is ≤ the first element of the right half. This ensures no 'dip' occurs at the boundary when merging. Step-by-step: 1. Input: array arr, indices leftEnd and rightStart representing the boundary. 2. Check condition: arr[leftEnd] ≤ arr[rightStart]. 3. Return true if condition holds, false otherwise. Time complexity is O(1) as it involves a single comparison. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L0: This check is used in the final step of L0 to combine results from recursive calls. Common mistakes: Comparing wrong indices (e.g., leftStart instead of leftEnd), or not handling cases where halves might not be sorted (but in this context, they are guaranteed sorted from recursion). Real code pattern: def boundarySorted(arr, leftEnd, rightStart): return arr[leftEnd] <= arr[rightStart]. This assumes leftEnd and rightStart are valid indices and halves are sorted.

examples.trace: Given arr = [1, 3, 5, 2, 4, 6]. leftEnd index = 2 → arr[2] = 5. rightStart index = 3 → arr[3] = 2. Check: 5 ≤ 2? No. Return false.
Given arr = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]. leftEnd index = 2 → arr[2] = 3. rightStart index = 3 → arr[3] = 4. Check: 3 ≤ 4? Yes. Return true.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Decomposition of Sorted Property
ladders.desc: Break down the problem of checking sorted array into smaller subproblems using recursion.
ladders.explanation: The central insight is that the property 'array is sorted' can be defined recursively: an array is sorted if its left half is sorted, its right half is sorted, and the boundary between them is sorted. This allows us to use a divide and conquer strategy. Intuition: Instead of checking every adjacent pair linearly, we split the array, check each half independently, and combine results with a single boundary check. This reduces the problem size exponentially until base cases are reached. Step-by-step: 1. Define recursive function that takes array and indices. 2. Base case: if subarray has 0 or 1 element, it's trivially sorted. 3. Recursive case: split into two halves, check each recursively, then check boundary. 4. Combine results using AND logic. Time complexity: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(1), which solves to O(n) via Master Theorem. Space complexity: O(log n) for recursion stack. Connection to L1: This logic uses the boundary check from L1 as the combining step. Common mistakes: Not defining proper base cases, infinite recursion, or incorrect splitting indices. Real code pattern: def isSortedRecursive(arr, start, end): if start >= end: return True; mid = (start + end) // 2; return isSortedRecursive(arr, start, mid) and isSortedRecursive(arr, mid+1, end) and (arr[mid] <= arr[mid+1]).

examples.trace: Call with start=0, end=2. Base case? start=0 < end=2, so proceed. mid = (0+2)//2 = 1. Recursively check left half: start=0, end=1. mid=0. Check left-left: start=0, end=0 → base case returns true. Check left-right: start=1, end=1 → base case returns true. Boundary check for left half: arr[0]=1 ≤ arr[1]=2 → true. So left half returns true. Recursively check right half: start=2, end=2 → base case returns true. Final boundary check: arr[1]=2 ≤ arr[2]=3 → true. Return true.

Call with start=0, end=2. mid=1. Check left half: start=0, end=1. mid=0. Check left-left: start=0, end=0 → true. Check left-right: start=1, end=1 → true. Boundary check for left half: arr[0]=3 ≤ arr[1]=1 → false. So left half returns false. Check right half: start=2, end=2 → true. Final boundary check not reached due to false. Return false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Array Splitting with Indices
ladders.desc: Split an array into halves recursively using start and end indices.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves dividing an array into smaller subarrays through recursion, which is fundamental to divide and conquer algorithms. The intuition is to avoid creating new arrays by using indices to represent subarrays, saving space. We define a function that takes the original array and two indices (start, end) inclusive, and recursively processes the left half (start to mid) and right half (mid+1 to end). Step-by-step: 1. Define function process(arr, start, end). 2. Base case: if start ≥ end, process trivially (e.g., return). 3. Calculate mid = (start + end) // 2. 4. Recursively call process(arr, start, mid) for left half. 5. Recursively call process(arr, mid+1, end) for right half. Time complexity depends on operations per call, but splitting itself is O(1) per call. Space complexity is O(log n) for recursion depth. Connection to L2: This splitting mechanism is used in L2 to decompose the sorted check problem. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in indices (e.g., using mid instead of mid+1 for right half), or not handling empty subarrays correctly. Real code pattern: def splitRecursive(arr, start, end): if start >= end: return; mid = (start + end) // 2; splitRecursive(arr, start, mid); splitRecursive(arr, mid+1, end). This pattern is the skeleton for many divide and conquer algorithms.

examples.trace: Call splitRecursive(arr, 0, 3). start=0 < end=3, so mid = (0+3)//2 = 1. Recursively call left: splitRecursive(arr, 0, 1). start=0 < end=1, mid=0. Left-left: splitRecursive(arr, 0, 0) → base case returns. Left-right: splitRecursive(arr, 1, 1) → base case returns. Back to first call, recursively call right: splitRecursive(arr, 2, 3). start=2 < end=3, mid=2. Right-left: splitRecursive(arr, 2, 2) → base case. Right-right: splitRecursive(arr, 3, 3) → base case. Done.
Call splitRecursive(arr, 0, 1). start=0 < end=1, mid=0. Left: splitRecursive(arr, 0, 0) → base case. Right: splitRecursive(arr, 1, 1) → base case. Done.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare Adjacent Elements in Array
ladders.desc: Check if one element is less than or equal to the next element in an array.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is the building block for checking sorted order. The intuition is that an array is sorted in non-decreasing order if and only if for every pair of adjacent elements, the first is ≤ the second. This single comparison captures the local sorted property between two elements. Step-by-step: 1. Given array arr and two consecutive indices i and i+1. 2. Access arr[i] and arr[i+1]. 3. Compare: arr[i] ≤ arr[i+1]. 4. Return true if condition holds, false otherwise. Time complexity is O(1), space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: This comparison is used in L3 when checking boundaries after splitting. Common mistakes: Using < instead of ≤ (which would fail for equal elements), or comparing non-adjacent indices. Real code pattern: def isOrdered(arr, i): return arr[i] <= arr[i+1]. This assumes i and i+1 are within array bounds.

examples.trace: Given arr = [1, 2]. i=0, so compare arr[0]=1 and arr[1]=2. Check: 1 ≤ 2? Yes. Return true.
Given arr = [5, 3]. i=0, compare arr[0]=5 and arr[1]=3. Check: 5 ≤ 3? No. Return false.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Recursion Base Case
ladders.desc: Learn the simplest case where recursion stops: empty or single-element arrays.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept for recursion: defining a base case that stops the recursive calls. For array problems, the simplest subproblems are when the array has 0 or 1 element. Intuition: An empty array or a single-element array is trivially sorted because there are no pairs to compare, so we can return true immediately. This prevents infinite recursion and provides a starting point for building up solutions. Step-by-step: 1. Define a function that checks if a subarray defined by start and end indices is base case. 2. Condition: if start ≥ end, then subarray has 0 or 1 element. 3. In this case, return true (or handle appropriately). Time and space complexity are O(1) for this check. Connection to L4: This base case is used in L4's recursive structure to terminate. Common mistakes: Forgetting base case leading to stack overflow, or incorrect condition (e.g., using > instead of ≥). Real code pattern: def baseCase(start, end): return start >= end. This returns true for base case, false otherwise.

examples.trace: start=0, end=0. Check: start ≥ end? 0 ≥ 0 is true. So it's a base case (single element). Return true.
start=0, end=2. Check: start ≥ end? 0 ≥ 2 is false. So it's not a base case (has multiple elements). Return false.
